topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the quiet finality of a closing door. My breath hitched in my chest, a shallow, involuntary catch. I reread the text three times, the words blurring slightly as my eyes tracked the familiar headline, only to realize the byline was utterly foreign. A coldness seeped into my limbs, making my hands tremble as I gripped the mouse. It wasn't just a mistake; it was a profound erasure of my effort, a theft of my voice. I leaned back in my chair, the worn leather groaning beneath me, and stared blankly at the wall, the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead sounding like a mocking chorus. A tightness constricted my throat, making swallowing feel like sandpaper. I slowly closed the laptop lid, the soft click echoing loudly in the silence of my apartment.

[story 2]

When I saw the published piece, a strange, hollow sensation settled deep in my stomach. My fingers hovered over the screen, unable to move forward, as if paralyzed by an invisible weight. The carefully crafted narrative I had poured my soul into was now someone else’s achievement, someone else’s acclaim. I let out a slow, ragged exhale, the air catching in my lungs with a dull ache. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to see the indifferent rush of city traffic below. Each passing car seemed to highlight the chasm between what I had created and what was now publicly attributed to another. A single tear escaped, tracing a hot, slow path down my cheek before I quickly wiped it away, my jaw clenched tight.

[story 3]

I found the notification tucked away in a folder, and the color seemed to drain from the room. My heart began to beat irregularly, a frantic, uneven rhythm against my ribs. I traced the unfamiliar name on the publication page with a fingertip, the texture of the screen feeling alien and wrong beneath my touch. A deep weariness washed over me, heavy and pervasive, making my eyelids feel impossibly heavy. I stood up abruptly, needing to move, but my legs felt unsteady, weighted down by an inexplicable inertia. I paced the small space between the desk and the bookshelf, my movements jerky and uncoordinated, muttering disjointed words under my breath, the sound barely audible above the hum of the refrigerator.
